speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is Mr. Joseph Greenblatt, tape four. So the message was given to Rozenfeld's brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Rozenfeld's brother brought it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took them both to-- to this-- to Pawel Frenkel, or Paul Frenkel, they would say it in English. And they saw the note. And he notified immediately [NON-ENGLISH] group. And an hour later, they got the order-- tomorrow, we fight. Take your positions. And I ordered my group [NON-ENGLISH] was my headquarters, to take positions. And in the morning, we heard the shots from the central ghetto. And they entered-- they tried to enter the ghetto, and the Ukrainian SS. And at first-- and they were greeted by a hail of bullets from SS uniforms. This was our stolen uniforms from the shops. They retreated and screaming in Ukrainian, we were this-- betrayed by the Germans. They were killing us, not the Jews. They retreated, and then they were sent back. They were still greeted this-- greeted with-- this we heard from the far. Between the central ghetto and where we were, at [NON-ENGLISH], was a no man's land. That's why our phone wires were going through the no man's land, through the sewer. And they were fighting till this-- I lost quite a number of very close friends. The Germans were shutting down from the outside of the ghetto. Down from the roofs, they pulled up. And the headquarters, [NON-ENGLISH], lower white flags and the Polish national flag. And it was a fortress. Every building over there was a fortress they were fighting. But I would say close to two weeks they were there. We were left alone for three days before they came to us. And we had a lot of battles prepared for them. [NON-ENGLISH] was paved with cobblestones. It's cobble bricks, actually, covered with tar. Once a Molotov bottle hid it, the tar started to burn together with the wooden paving. So it was another retreat. Some were burnt. Then the next time they came, we aimed the bol-- bottles directly at them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'd throw behind them and in the front of them, then in the center. I had a girl, Cypora Szapszyk. She was sitting on a rooftop and was throwing one grenade, one Molotov bottle. That girl was fantastic. Really fantastic. She-- help couldn't get to her. So whatever she had, she was throwing. And that's how she survived, and then she was able to get down when they moved away. Their fire, which they were throwing on the building, stopped. Then she went. She came down and took another supply and said, no, I'll sit on there. That's the best place.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was marvelous. Then we had also, from the [NON-ENGLISH] side, we were attacking them back. When the fight stopped-- one evening, we were relaxing in one of the apartments on the ground floor. And two [NON-ENGLISH] officers went in. They managed to hide us under the beds, and we captured them. We disarmed them. Took their uniforms down. In the meantime, we were contacted by the central ghetto, with Paul Frenkel's headquarters, which was my headquarters, too, that they have to withdraw. They're withdrawing. They said, you'll be notified. Fight as long as you can, and the help will come to you. And we fought till Palm Sunday. Palm Sunday, we went down till somebody came to contact us. We started to move down to the sewer. We had a underground channel to the sewer, and we were moving out. We walked five hours in the sewer. They went down with 20 some-- 20-- 28 people. They came out six, because the others were drowned in the sewer. They were drug-- poison gas. People were drowning. We came out six, and we marched-- like I said, five or six hours in the sewer. Men down with two sticks chasing the water rats. We came out on the outskirts of Warsaw, in Wolska. It was an outskirt. And we were transported to a forest out of Warsaw, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from the Polish underground. The AK. That was it, three groups. So I'll talk later about them. The AK, they knew the way. It was pretty organized. Of course, we were buying the arms from them. We were good customers. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Go hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- this-- we [NON-ENGLISH]. And one of them-- we were transferred over there. We were there a few days. Met with this-- with the ghetto group. Pawel and the others. And I had to go to Warsaw to see my wife, where she was hiding. And when I came back the following day, no one was there. No signs. And I knew where they-- the other place where the-- the reserved place where it will be on [NON-ENGLISH] 17, across from the all-Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]. And when I got over there, there was a fight from the outside, where they were attacked by the Germans. And it was the first battle, and the whole group was wiped out. A lot of Nazis killed over there. And I left them, and I made it on my own. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My contact from the Polish underground, with whom I escaped together, from prison war camp, and they took me with open arms. I went in with a false name, as Jan Bednarczyk. They knew my background, so they gave me right away my rank back. And I was with them. The whole time, we participated in several actions. Polish-- the Armia Krajowa was one of the best organized. They worked with the Polish exile government in London, with the Sikorski group. As you know, there were three different organizations in Poland. One was the PAL, the Polish folks' army. And there was AL, the army-- the folks' army. One was more left-- and the AL was strictly communist group. The one was something in between. And then this was the main organization, was the army of the land, of which I was a member. Many-- I participated in many actions. The most important action, which I was-- got decorated for it, was the execution of Himmler's brother in law, Kuczera. Kuczera took charge of the police at the Gestapo in Warsaw in the beginning of 1944. And he was responsible for thousands of deaths. He just had all the-- hanged them. Hanged them on this terrace, hanged them on this terr-- many priests. And they decide to liquidate him. I was in charge of the retreat group, to cover the retreats. One day, it was June. I don't remember the date if you kill me. The date was set that Kuczera has to be liquidated. We knew that he will take a stroll in a car-- in an open car on the Poniatowski bridge, which connected to the outskirts of Praga with central Warsaw. And we were supposed to liquidate him on that bridge, because he was surrounded by Gestapo from all the sides. And we-- as he came, it was around noon time. He came over the bridge. [NON-ENGLISH], who was-- Wacek, who was in charge of the execution, managed to get very close to his car, and put two bullets in him and killed him on the spot. But also, he paid with his life for it. And, of course, the entire area was surrounded immediately by Gestapo and SS men, and I was arrested. I only had in my pocket two hand grenades and two guns. They didn't arrest me as a participant, only as a-- I don't know, why-- maybe-- maybe I'm involved with-- hundreds of Poles. Put me in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And [INAUDIBLE] but 30 or 40 and pushed into this. I dispose in the wagon of the guns and of the grenades. Although, it didn't mean anything, if they'll discover-- if they'll take-- they'll get me into the Gestapo. If I have the grenades or not, I'll be shot, because the first thing they said, pull down your pants. Excuse my expressions. But anyhow, I had a habit. I always had in my pocket a swastika. For two purposes. First of all, I felt much more comfortable riding with the German in the front of the platform, the front platform, then with the Poles who could recognize me. After all, I was raised in War-- in Warsaw. All day, they could feel out a Jew through three pair of underwear. With them, I mean, a Volksdeutsche who doesn't speak German, it was normal. But they called them [NON-ENGLISH]. They called the Volksdeutsche. And felt-- so I had that swastika in my pocket. So once they put us in a room for interrogation, I started to canvas the ro-- the room. I opened one door. It's a broom closet. I open another door, it was some other garbage there. Finally, I open the door and I see Ukrainian SSes sitting there. And there's a door leading to the next street. From [NON-ENGLISH] they are back to back. I said, that will be-- that's my escape route. I closed a bit the door, put the swastika on, and went in and started to curse them in German. They didn't understand. One word, my German also was a lot to desire. I started cursing [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. And they say, [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, I'll be back in 10 minutes. I want to have that room sparkling clean. And I said, heil Hitler, and went out through the other door. That's how I vanished. I was a free man again. Because everything-- what we were doing in those days is without thinking. Their actions worked faster than the brains. Because we would think we would never do it. So every action, every move, was faster than the brains. Although they say the brains work much faster. It's not true. Not in our case, anyhow. Then again, I think that's why I got the medal on the Polish-- I got the medal for bravery and two bars. That was the highest medal for bravery, the two bars. And my name remained Jan Bednarczyk. That's how I worked them still the end of the war. Then come the Polish uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- my group was in the old city Warsaw. That's where the first battle was. And we were also the first group to be evacuated from over there, to central Warsaw. We had to go through a tunnel, cut in the middle of the streets. Because the German was on the sides. We cut through a tunnel in the middle. They took us by.

speaker: interviewee
text: After we passed, the tunnel was in that area where Kuczera was killed. And I was in the central part of Warsaw, [NON-ENGLISH]. And then we fought until all of Warsaw had to be cleared. Everyone, the walls collapsed. It's not like the war to Warsaw ghetto. You go back out of it, the Warsaw ghetto never collapsed. There was no surrender, no white flags. It was just the blue white flag waving in our ears like the 'Hatikvah' song, with a song of hope, it sound in our ears. [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The Poles surrender. There were white flags. And Warsaw had to be evacuated. No one was allowed to live in Warsaw. But another few Jews, among them, from [NON-ENGLISH], Goldstein, Bernard Goldstein. And David Guzik from the Joint-- from Joint. To me, I had a quarrel about his loyalty to us. But he got killed too early, and I couldn't substantiate. In my opinion, he was a collaborator. But-- we had a bunker on [NON-ENGLISH] on the gymnasium, which was before the war a gymnasium for girls. We built over there a bunker on the half-destroyed building. We were there quite a while, from Novem-- September-- I think from the month of September till-- about a month we were there. Three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: September, '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, till about three weeks we were there. Then we discovered that the Nazis were sitting on the top of us. And they opened an office over there, and we said-- and then one night, we heard that they are crawling over there in the basements. So we decided to evacuate ourselves as fast as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: They stood over there, over the next day. That was actually in the neighborhood where I was born and lived till I went to the ghetto. That's [NON-ENGLISH]. And we-- evacuate us. They wanted to evacuate, but to where? So I said, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, we will find some basement or something, one of the burnt houses. Because the Nazis were going around every night and burning the houses. What wasn't burnt during the war was destroyed actually during the-- after the Polish uprising collapsed. They were destroying building by building.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since we were going at night to look for food, we were also looking for an escape, where to escape. So finally, we found [NON-ENGLISH] 19. That was [NON-ENGLISH], where now is the hotel built. And was that-- at night, in the day, we will be underground, sleeping at night. Will go up on the-- on the burnt down roof to get fresh air. And this, and there we were till the liberation. One day, we heard-- we were sitting over there, and we heard Polish. It's all because [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was a a-- not a dirty word about Russians, but a-- a-- not a complimentary expression. [NON-ENGLISH], that there are no more [NON-ENGLISH] here. [NON-ENGLISH] come actually from something, but they had to do with Cossacks, so what they-- but it wasn't a bad word.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russians. They were staying the other side of Warsaw, in Praga, several months. [INAUDIBLE]. They wanted Poles to be wiped out, Polish resistance. Because it was easier during the war for them to cross the-- on the Vistula. There was no resistance, almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were taking, during the day, the revolt in the ghetto over bunker, which I prepared for them. My parents, my brother, and my sister. And some neighbors. My parents, my sister, and my brother were taken to Treblinka. Treblinka? Treblinka. And there, my father and my brother were cham-- gas chambered over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said my father and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what happened to mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother and my sister were taken, then, to-- transferred to Auschwitz from Majdanek, from Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was killed. Was killed. And my sister wanted to go with her, but she was hit by Mengele with the whip over that-- she's always had a mark here-- and pushed back to the line. Then a few months later, my sister was taken, that's what she told me, into the gas chamber. And she spent a night over there. See, I lost the sequence. Now I have to go back. She spent the night over there. In the morning, they let them out. But they thought-- the rumor was they ran out-- from the-- which they heard from the sonderkommando-- that they ran out of Zyklon. And Mengele said, they had their bath, and they they're out. So-- and said, not that group has to go in, but another group. So they let them out. Then came the evacuation of Auschwitz. And at the other evacuation, my sister escaped. That was the evacuation from Birkenau to Ravensbruck. And she covered herself with a dead body and ditched-- and jumped into a ditch. Actually, she jumped into a ditch and covered herself with the dead body. And that's how she survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- come on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she met her future husband, who also escaped. And they fell into the American hands, and she wound up with her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she wound up in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after-- you after liberation? How did you find your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew where she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew where she was, and you went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go afterwards together? Did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was remobilized to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I saw the-- the parts and the battles, the Polish army. And on the ground, when I got my medal, but also nominated as a captain. And as a captain, I was mobilized. Not as a Jew, because they ordered all the reserve officers from the Polish army, and all the officers, actually, from the Polish army are mobilized immediately, we report to the nearest military unit. So I didn't go at the beginning. But my group from the land-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the land of the army did give out the list of the officers. They surrendered. There was a battle of one surrendered, one group did not surrender. They was waiting for the orders from London. My group surrendered with the list. So they didn't take me as Jan-- Joseph Greenblatt, but as Jan Bednarczyk, who was the son of John and Maria [NON-ENGLISH], born in [NON-ENGLISH]. My mother was [NON-ENGLISH] and the village of [NON-ENGLISH]. It's the outskirt of Warsaw. They took me as-- and I went as Captain Jan Bednarczyk into the army. Then I was military commander after the-- after the war ended, I was the military commander of the city of Gliwice, was occupied territory. It's the annexed territory. They call it liberated territory, because at one time it was Poland. And I was in charge of the city for Polish questions. And I was also signing the documents for the so-called Greek Jews who were interned in Poland in the concentration camps and returning home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this, I used to sign for them. [INAUDIBLE]. I have a cousin in Israel now, in the [NON-ENGLISH], who met-- [NON-ENGLISH] about whom I was talking before. She met her on the ship from Italy to Israel, and they started to talk each other, and [NON-ENGLISH], my cousin, said you were in Betar in Warsaw? Said, my-- I had a cousin who was one of the leaders of the Betar in Warsaw. His name is-- they said, what was his name? They said, [NON-ENGLISH] Greenblatt. So he said, if not for [NON-ENGLISH] Greenblatt, you wouldn't be here. You come over there, and you'll see, signed you this by Captain Jan Bednarczyk. That is your cousin. When we were sitting, my parents last day in Lodz, in the house. He was sitting in the other room, signing-- he came special from Gliwice to sign the documents. Only we couldn't see him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could see him because I was-- I knew him from when we were kids together, in Betar. I knew him, so parents wanted to show off that I am-- that he is alive, he does something. [NON-ENGLISH] was the man who took charge of Betar in Poland after the whole war was finished. [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. They took charge, and they were leading the Bricha of the Betar groups. And that's how she knew that I am alive, and that's how we made contact. And as a matter of fact, the same [NON-ENGLISH]. I met her right after war when she come out of the underground. Because she was arrested. She was caught, and she was sent to Auschwitz, and she saw my sister. And she told me, [NON-ENGLISH], I'm sure your sister is alive. Because she escaped from the column. And that's how I found my sister. Then one day, I went with my sister-in-law, the one who is now in Australia, to the Jewish community-- I was still in uniform-- to find out if anyone is alive. And I gave up hope. She asked if anyone was looking for her, and she didn't have any-- and I said, by the way, let me look. By chance, maybe somebody look for me. And it was a cable from my sister in Belgium, that she's looking for Joseph Greenblatt. [SOBBING] He was in a Polish underground, and I hope that he survived. But they didn't have anything of--

speaker: interviewer
text: No record with that name.

speaker: interviewee
text: With that name. I never registered. And I make-- and they brought that telegram. In short, not among the living. I make a-- [INAUDIBLE] said, why should we look for you? Who will know that you're Jan Bednarczyk? Then they cabled my sister to Belgium that I'm alive. And then, of course, when I escaped Poland, I escaped Poland in a uniform with a military Jeep, went with a false-- this order of traveling. And I'm going on military business to East Berlin with my secretary, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to take a break.
